answer:
React Hooks at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

React Hooks enable function components to manage state, side effects, and lifecycle without classes. At 1M+ users, scalable Hook architecture focuses on: custom hook composition for business logic reuse, memoization strategies to prevent unnecessary re-renders, state colocation (keeping state close to where it's used), and hook dependency management to avoid stale closure bugs. Key patterns include extracted custom hooks for data fetching, form management, and real-time subscriptions.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub's React 18 frontend managed 10,000+ contacts with real-time search, bulk operations, and offline support. Initially, each component independently called useState and useEffect for API data, causing 15+ redundant API calls on the contacts page. Manoj extracted custom hooks: useContacts(filters) (React Query wrapper with optimistic updates), useBulkSelection() (shared selection state via context), and useOfflineSync() (PWA service worker integration). This reduced API calls from 15 to 3 (deduplication via React Query), enabled offline-first rendering, and cut the contacts page render time from 120ms to 35ms.

📊 Custom Hook Architecture

💻 Code Example — Scalable Custom Hooks

// Custom hook: useContacts with React Query
function useContacts(filters: ContactFilters) {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  const query = useQuery({
    queryKey: ['contacts', filters],
    queryFn: () => api.getContacts(filters),
    staleTime: 30_000, // 30s cache
    placeholderData: keepPreviousData, // Smooth pagination
  });

  // Optimistic create
  const createMutation = useMutation({
    mutationFn: api.createContact,
    onMutate: async (newContact) => {
      await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['contacts'] });
      const prev = queryClient.getQueryData(['contacts', filters]);
      queryClient.setQueryData(['contacts', filters], (old) => ({
        ...old,
        items: [{ ...newContact, id: 'temp-' + Date.now() }, ...old.items]
      }));
      return { prev };
    },
    onError: (_err, _new, context) => {
      queryClient.setQueryData(['contacts', filters], context.prev);
    },
    onSettled: () => {
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['contacts'] });
    }
  });

  return { ...query, createContact: createMutation.mutate };
}

// Custom hook: useBulkSelection
function useBulkSelection(items: T[]) {
  const [selectedIds, setSelectedIds] = useState>(new Set());

  const toggleItem = useCallback((id: string) => {
    setSelectedIds(prev => {
      const next = new Set(prev);
      next.has(id) ? next.delete(id) : next.add(id);
      return next;
    });
  }, []);

  const selectAll = useCallback(() => {
    setSelectedIds(new Set(items.map(i => i.id)));
  }, [items]);

  const clearSelection = useCallback(() => setSelectedIds(new Set()), []);

  const selectedItems = useMemo(
    () => items.filter(i => selectedIds.has(i.id)),
    [items, selectedIds]
  );

  return { selectedIds, selectedItems, toggleItem, selectAll, clearSelection };
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

ContactsHub's contact list showed stale data after creating a new contact. React DevTools showed the useContacts hook wasn't refetching after mutation. The root cause: the mutation's onSettled callback invalidated ['contacts'] but the query key included filters — ['contacts', filters]. Since filters was a new object reference each render (created inline), React Query treated every render's query as unique. Manoj stabilized the filters object with useMemo and used partial query key matching in invalidation: queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['contacts'] }) (without exact match). He added a React Query devtools panel to development builds for inspecting query cache state.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Unstable dependency references in hooksInfinite re-render loops or stale closuresStabilize objects with useMemo; use primitive query keys
Missing cleanup in useEffectMemory leaks, zombie subscriptionsAlways return cleanup function; use AbortController for fetch
Over-using useState for derived dataState sync bugs; redundant re-rendersDerive from existing state with useMemo; avoid duplicate state
Custom hooks without memoized returnsNew object reference each render triggers child re-rendersMemoize returned objects; use useMemo for computed values

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Hook testing: renderHook from Testing Library; test state transitions and cleanup
  • React DevTools Profiler: Render count per component < 3 per user action
  • Query cache inspection: React Query DevTools verifying deduplication and cache hits
  • Memory leak test: Mount/unmount 100 times; heap snapshot shows no growth
  • Playwright E2E: Create contact → verify appears in list within 200ms (optimistic update)

React Hooks — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

React Hook performance bottlenecks include: excessive re-renders from unstable references, stale closure bugs causing incorrect behavior, useEffect dependency array mistakes triggering infinite loops, and context-based state updates re-rendering entire subtrees. Instrumentation uses React DevTools Profiler, why-did-you-render library, custom performance marks, and render-count tracking to identify and resolve hook-related performance issues.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub's search feature re-rendered the entire 10,000-contact list on every keystroke. React DevTools Profiler showed ContactList rendering 300ms per keystroke due to unstable filter objects passed as props. Manoj instrumented with why-did-you-render which pinpointed: the useContacts hook returned a new object reference each render, causing all child components to re-render. He memoized the hook's return value, added useDeferredValue for search input, and wrapped ContactRow in React.memo. Search render time dropped from 300ms to 15ms with zero visual lag.

📊 Hook Performance Optimization Flow

💻 Code Example — Optimized Hook Performance

// ❌ Before: unstable reference from hook
function useContacts(filters) {
  const { data } = useQuery(['contacts', filters], fetchContacts);
  // Returns new object every render!
  return { contacts: data?.items ?? [], total: data?.total ?? 0 };
}

// ✅ After: memoized stable reference
function useContacts(filters) {
  const { data } = useQuery(['contacts', filters], fetchContacts);
  return useMemo(() => ({
    contacts: data?.items ?? [],
    total: data?.total ?? 0
  }), [data]);
}

// Deferred search input — non-blocking UI
function ContactSearch() {
  const [search, setSearch] = useState('');
  const deferredSearch = useDeferredValue(search);
  const { contacts } = useContacts({ search: deferredSearch });

  return (
    <>
       setSearch(e.target.value)}
        placeholder="Search contacts..."
      />
      {/* List renders with deferred value — input stays responsive */}
      
    
  );
}

// Memoized row component
const ContactRow = React.memo(({ contact }: { contact: Contact }) => (
  

    {contact.name}
    {contact.email}
  

));

// Development instrumentation
if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development') {
  const whyDidYouRender = require('@welldone-software/why-did-you-render');
  whyDidYouRender(React, { trackAllPureComponents: true });
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After deploying the search feature, users on low-end devices reported the input "freezing" when typing quickly. React DevTools Profiler showed the ContactList component rendering for 300ms on each keystroke — blocking the main thread. why-did-you-render identified the cause: useContacts returned a new object reference even when the underlying data hadn't changed, forcing all 10,000 ContactRow components to re-render. Three fixes applied: (1) useMemo on the hook return to stabilize the reference, (2) useDeferredValue on the search string to deprioritize list updates, (3) React.memo on ContactRow to skip unchanged rows. Combined, these reduced the render from 300ms to 15ms — well within the 16ms frame budget.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Hooks returning new objects each renderAll consumers re-render unnecessarilyWrap return values in useMemo; return stable references
useMemo/useCallback with wrong depsStale data or unnecessary recalculationseslint-plugin-react-hooks exhaustive-deps rule
Context updates re-rendering entire treeO(n) re-renders for single state changeSplit contexts by update frequency; use useSyncExternalStore
useDeferredValue hiding stale UIUsers see outdated data brieflyShow loading indicator while deferred value is stale

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Profiler budget: No component render > 16ms in React DevTools Profiler
  • Render count: useRenderCount hook in dev; ContactRow renders ≤ 1 per data change
  • why-did-you-render: Zero unnecessary re-renders flagged in dev builds
  • Input responsiveness: Playwright test: type 20 chars in search → input delay < 50ms
  • Memory check: Heap snapshot stable after 100 search queries (no leaked closures)

React Rendering — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

React Rendering performance bottlenecks stem from: unnecessary reconciliation (diffing unchanged subtrees), large component trees blocking the main thread, synchronous rendering preventing user interaction, and inefficient virtual DOM operations. React 18's concurrent features (Suspense, transitions, streaming SSR) address many of these, but require careful architecture. Instrumentation uses React DevTools Profiler, browser Performance API, Interaction to Next Paint (INP), and custom render timing to identify bottlenecks.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub rendered a virtualized list of 10,000+ contacts with real-time search filtering. Initial profiling showed 45ms reconciliation time when filters changed — React diffed all 10,000 virtual DOM nodes even though only 50 were visible. Manoj implemented react-window for virtualization (rendering only visible rows), startTransition for non-urgent filter updates, and Suspense boundaries for progressive loading. Reconciliation dropped to 3ms (only 50 visible rows), and INP improved from 380ms to 45ms.

📊 React Rendering Pipeline Optimization

💻 Code Example — Concurrent Rendering with Virtualization

import { FixedSizeList } from 'react-window';
import { startTransition, Suspense, useDeferredValue } from 'react';

function ContactsPage() {
  const [search, setSearch] = useState('');
  const deferredSearch = useDeferredValue(search);
  const { contacts } = useContacts({ search: deferredSearch });

  const handleSearch = (e: React.ChangeEvent) => {
    // Urgent: update input immediately
    setSearch(e.target.value);
    // Non-urgent: filter results can be deferred
  };

  return (
    

      
      }>
        
      
    

  );
}

// Virtualized list — only renders visible rows
function VirtualizedContactList({ contacts }: { contacts: Contact[] }) {
  const Row = useCallback(({ index, style }: { index: number; style: React.CSSProperties }) => (
    

      
    

  ), [contacts]);

  return (
    
      {Row}
    
  );
}

// Profiler wrapper for instrumentation
function ProfiledContactList({ contacts }) {
  return (
     {
        if (actualDuration > 16) {
          console.warn(`${id} slow render: ${actualDuration.toFixed(1)}ms (${phase})`);
        }
        analytics.track('render_timing', { component: id, duration: actualDuration, phase });
      }}
    >
      
    
  );
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After adding tag-based filtering to ContactsHub, users reported the UI "janking" when switching between tags. Chrome DevTools Performance tab showed long tasks of 380ms blocking the main thread during reconciliation. React DevTools Profiler revealed: all 10,000 ContactRow components were being diffed even though only 50 were visible in the viewport. Manoj implemented react-window for virtualization (only 50 rows in the DOM), wrapped filter changes in startTransition (allowing React to interrupt rendering for user input), and added Suspense for progressive loading. The Profiler showed reconciliation time dropped from 45ms to 3ms, and web-vitals INP improved from 380ms to 45ms.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Rendering large lists without virtualizationDOM has 10,000+ nodes; reconciliation blocks main threadUse react-window or react-virtuoso; only render visible items
All state updates treated as urgentInput feels laggy when rendering is slowstartTransition for non-urgent updates; useDeferredValue
Over-memoizing (useMemo everywhere)Memory overhead; harder to debug; marginal benefitOnly memoize expensive computations; profile first, optimize second
Suspense without fallback UIUser sees nothing while loadingAlways provide meaningful skeleton/fallback for Suspense boundaries

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Profiler API: Custom onRender callback; alert if any component > 16ms
  • Web Vitals: INP < 100ms target; measured via web-vitals library in production
  • Lighthouse CI: TBT < 200ms, LCP < 2.5s on simulated mobile
  • Virtualization check: DOM node count < 200 regardless of list size
  • Playwright perf test: Filter 10,000 contacts → assert visible update < 100ms

React Rendering — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

React's rendering model involves: (1) render phase (calling component functions, creating virtual DOM), (2) reconciliation (diffing old vs. new virtual DOM), and (3) commit phase (applying DOM mutations). An interviewer-grade understanding covers: the fiber architecture enabling concurrent rendering, how React batches state updates, the reconciliation algorithm's O(n) heuristics (same type = update, different type = remount), key prop's role in list reconciliation, and the trade-offs between React's declarative model and direct DOM manipulation for performance-critical UIs.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's CRM Dashboard

The CRM Dashboard's sales pipeline used drag-and-drop to move leads between stages (Kanban board). During drag, React re-rendered the entire board (200+ cards) on every mouse move event (60 FPS = 60 renders/second). The reconciliation phase couldn't keep up, causing visible jank. Manoj implemented a hybrid approach: React managed the board layout and data, but drag animations used direct DOM manipulation via useRef + CSS transforms (bypassing React's render cycle). The drag position updated via requestAnimationFrame directly on the DOM element, while React only re-rendered on drop (data commit). This achieved smooth 60 FPS dragging with zero React renders during the drag interaction.

📊 React Rendering Pipeline Deep Dive

💻 Code Example — Hybrid React + Direct DOM Rendering

// Kanban card with hybrid rendering approach
function KanbanCard({ lead, onDrop }: { lead: Lead; onDrop: (stage: string) => void }) {
  const cardRef = useRef(null);
  const dragOffset = useRef({ x: 0, y: 0 });
  const isDragging = useRef(false);

  const handlePointerDown = useCallback((e: React.PointerEvent) => {
    isDragging.current = true;
    const rect = cardRef.current!.getBoundingClientRect();
    dragOffset.current = { x: e.clientX - rect.left, y: e.clientY - rect.top };

    // Direct DOM: bypass React for 60fps drag
    const handleMove = (e: PointerEvent) => {
      if (!isDragging.current) return;
      requestAnimationFrame(() => {
        if (cardRef.current) {
          cardRef.current.style.transform =
            `translate(${e.clientX - dragOffset.current.x}px, ${e.clientY - dragOffset.current.y}px)`;
          cardRef.current.style.position = 'fixed';
          cardRef.current.style.zIndex = '1000';
        }
      });
    };

    const handleUp = (e: PointerEvent) => {
      isDragging.current = false;
      // Reset direct DOM styles
      if (cardRef.current) {
        cardRef.current.style.transform = '';
        cardRef.current.style.position = '';
        cardRef.current.style.zIndex = '';
      }
      // NOW trigger React re-render via state update
      const dropTarget = document.elementFromPoint(e.clientX, e.clientY)
        ?.closest('[data-stage]')?.getAttribute('data-stage');
      if (dropTarget) onDrop(dropTarget);

      document.removeEventListener('pointermove', handleMove);
      document.removeEventListener('pointerup', handleUp);
    };

    document.addEventListener('pointermove', handleMove);
    document.addEventListener('pointerup', handleUp);
  }, [onDrop]);

  // React only renders on data change, not during drag
  return (
    

      
{lead.name}

      {lead.stage}
      ${lead.value.toLocaleString()}
    

  );
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

The CRM Kanban board dropped to 15 FPS during drag operations. Chrome DevTools Performance tab showed repeated "Recalculate Style" and "Layout" events triggered by React's DOM mutations during reconciliation. Each mouse move event triggered setState → render → reconciliation → DOM commit for 200+ cards. Manoj's hybrid approach eliminated React from the drag loop entirely: pointermove events directly updated CSS transforms via requestAnimationFrame, and React only re-rendered on pointerup (the drop). FPS jumped to 60 during drag. He added a Playwright test that measured FPS during drag operations using the Performance Observer API.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Direct DOM manipulation conflicting with ReactReact overwrites manual DOM changes on next renderOnly modify styles React doesn't control; reset before state update
Missing key prop on list itemsO(n) reconciliation instead of O(1) reorderUse stable, unique keys (DB IDs, not array indices)
State updates during animation framesReact batching delays visual feedbackUse refs for animation state; only commit via setState on completion
Reconciliation of deeply nested treesExpensive diffing even if leaf nodes unchangedUse React.memo at subtree boundaries; flatten component trees

✅ Validation Strategy

  • FPS monitoring: requestAnimationFrame counter during drag; assert ≥ 55 FPS
  • Render count during drag: React Profiler must show 0 renders during drag motion
  • INP measurement: web-vitals library tracking INP < 100ms for all interactions
  • Playwright drag test: Drag card across 3 stages → assert no frame drops (Performance Observer)
  • DOM node count: < 500 nodes for Kanban board regardless of lead count

Context vs State Library — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Context vs State Library performance bottlenecks center on React Context's fundamental limitation: any context value change re-renders ALL consumers, regardless of which part of the value they use. State libraries (Zustand, Jotai, Redux Toolkit) provide selector-based subscriptions that only re-render when the selected slice changes. Instrumentation involves render-count tracking, Context consumer profiling, and selector efficiency measurement to choose the right tool for each state domain.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub initially used React Context for all shared state: user auth, contact list, filters, selection, and UI preferences. When a user toggled dark mode (UI preference), every component consuming any part of the context re-rendered — including the 10,000-row contact list. React DevTools showed 250+ component re-renders for a single theme toggle. Manoj split state into domains: auth (Context — rarely changes), UI preferences (Context — rarely changes, separate from data), and contact data + filters (Zustand — changes frequently, needs selectors). Re-renders for theme toggle dropped from 250+ to 12 (only UI-consuming components).

📊 State Architecture — Context vs Library

💻 Code Example — Zustand Selectors vs Context

// ❌ Context: all consumers re-render on any state change
const AppContext = createContext<{
  user: User;
  contacts: Contact[];
  theme: 'light' | 'dark';
  filters: FilterState;
}>(null!);

// Every component using useContext(AppContext) re-renders
// when contacts change, even if it only reads theme

// ✅ Zustand: selector-based subscriptions
import { create } from 'zustand';

interface ContactStore {
  contacts: Contact[];
  filters: FilterState;
  selectedIds: Set;
  setContacts: (contacts: Contact[]) => void;
  setFilter: (key: string, value: any) => void;
  toggleSelect: (id: string) => void;
}

const useContactStore = create((set) => ({
  contacts: [],
  filters: { search: '', tags: [], sortBy: 'name' },
  selectedIds: new Set(),
  setContacts: (contacts) => set({ contacts }),
  setFilter: (key, value) => set((s) => ({
    filters: { ...s.filters, [key]: value }
  })),
  toggleSelect: (id) => set((s) => {
    const next = new Set(s.selectedIds);
    next.has(id) ? next.delete(id) : next.add(id);
    return { selectedIds: next };
  }),
}));

// Component only re-renders when contacts change
function ContactList() {
  const contacts = useContactStore(s => s.contacts); // Selector!
  return ;
}

// Component only re-renders when selectedIds change
function SelectionBar() {
  const count = useContactStore(s => s.selectedIds.size);
  return 
{count} selected
;
}

// Context remains for low-frequency state
const AuthContext = createContext<{ user: User; logout: () => void }>(null!);
const ThemeContext = createContext<{ theme: 'light' | 'dark'; toggle: () => void }>(null!);

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Users reported the ContactsHub interface "freezing" for 500ms when toggling dark mode. React DevTools Profiler showed 250+ components re-rendering. The root cause: all state was in a single AppContext, and changing theme invalidated the entire context value (reference comparison), forcing every useContext(AppContext) consumer to re-render. Manoj's fix: (1) Separated theme into its own ThemeContext (12 consumers), (2) Moved contact data to Zustand with selectors (only re-renders when selected slice changes), (3) Kept auth in its own AuthContext (changes only on login/logout). Post-fix, theme toggle re-rendered only 12 components, and contact list operations no longer triggered theme-consuming components.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Single monolithic Context for all stateEvery state change re-renders entire appSplit by domain and update frequency; max 3-4 contexts
Zustand selectors returning new objectsSelector creates new reference → re-render despite same dataUse shallow equality check: useStore(selector, shallow)
Mixing Context and state library for same domainConfusing data flow, sync issuesOne source of truth per domain; document which tool owns what
Over-engineering: Redux for simple stateBoilerplate overhead, slower developmentZustand for most cases; Redux only if you need middleware/devtools/persistence

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Render count per action: React Profiler tracking renders per state change; theme toggle < 15
  • Selector efficiency: Zustand devtools verifying selectors return stable references
  • Context split audit: No context provider wrapping > 50 components for frequently-changing state
  • State ownership doc: Architecture diagram showing which tool owns each state domain
  • Performance regression: Playwright test: toggle theme 10x → assert total frame drops = 0

Context vs State Library — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

Context vs State Library is a fundamental React architecture decision. React Context provides built-in dependency injection with a re-render-all-consumers model. State libraries (Zustand, Jotai, Redux Toolkit, Recoil) add subscription-based updates with granular re-rendering. An interviewer-grade understanding covers: why Context re-renders all consumers (reference equality on value prop), how libraries achieve granular updates (external stores with useSyncExternalStore), the mental model differences (top-down propagation vs. subscription), and when each approach is the right architectural choice.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's CRM Dashboard

The CRM Dashboard needed to share state across 6 independent widgets: pipeline data, filter state, selected lead, sort preferences, real-time notifications, and user preferences. Manoj evaluated: Context (simple but re-renders all widgets on any change), Redux Toolkit (powerful but heavy boilerplate for 6 widgets), and Zustand (lightweight, selector-based, minimal boilerplate). He chose Zustand for pipeline/filter/selection state (changes frequently, widgets need granular subscriptions) and Context for user preferences (changes rarely, needs to be available everywhere). This hybrid approach kept each widget's render count to < 3 per user interaction.

📊 Decision Framework: Context vs State Library

💻 Code Example — Hybrid Architecture

// Zustand store for frequently-changing CRM state
import { create } from 'zustand';
import { devtools, subscribeWithSelector } from 'zustand/middleware';

interface CRMStore {
  pipeline: Lead[];
  selectedLeadId: string | null;
  filters: { stage: string[]; assignee: string; search: string };
  selectLead: (id: string) => void;
  moveLead: (id: string, stage: string) => void;
}

const useCRMStore = create()(
  devtools(
    subscribeWithSelector((set) => ({
      pipeline: [],
      selectedLeadId: null,
      filters: { stage: [], assignee: '', search: '' },
      selectLead: (id) => set({ selectedLeadId: id }),
      moveLead: (id, stage) => set((s) => ({
        pipeline: s.pipeline.map(l =>
          l.id === id ? { ...l, stage } : l
        )
      })),
    })),
    { name: 'CRM Store' }
  )
);

// Widget 1: only re-renders when pipeline changes
function PipelineWidget() {
  const leads = useCRMStore(s => s.pipeline);
  return ;
}

// Widget 2: only re-renders when selected lead changes
function LeadDetail() {
  const leadId = useCRMStore(s => s.selectedLeadId);
  const lead = useCRMStore(s => s.pipeline.find(l => l.id === s.selectedLeadId));
  if (!lead) return ;
  return ;
}

// Context for rarely-changing user preferences
const UserPrefsContext = createContext<{
  theme: 'light' | 'dark';
  density: 'compact' | 'comfortable';
  toggleTheme: () => void;
}>(null!);

function UserPrefsProvider({ children }) {
  const [prefs, setPrefs] = useState({ theme: 'light', density: 'comfortable' });
  const toggleTheme = useCallback(() =>
    setPrefs(p => ({ ...p, theme: p.theme === 'light' ? 'dark' : 'light' })), []);
  
  const value = useMemo(() => ({ ...prefs, toggleTheme }), [prefs, toggleTheme]);
  return {children};
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In the CRM Dashboard, clicking a lead in the pipeline should highlight it and show details in the side panel — a simple selectedLeadId change. With Context, this triggered re-renders in all 6 widgets (pipeline, revenue, forecast, team, activity, detail). With Zustand selectors, only the Pipeline widget (to update highlight) and Lead Detail widget (to show the selected lead) re-rendered — 2 components instead of 6. Manoj verified this using React DevTools Profiler: the Zustand approach rendered 2 components in 4ms, while the Context approach rendered 6 components in 18ms. He documented the state ownership architecture in a team wiki, mapping each state domain to its tool and justification.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Context value not memoizedProvider re-render creates new value → all consumers re-renderAlways useMemo the context value object
Zustand store too granular (many small stores)Hard to manage; cross-store coordination complexOne store per feature domain; use slices pattern for large stores
Testing Zustand storesStore persists between testsReset store in beforeEach; use store factory pattern for tests
SSR hydration mismatch with external storesServer and client state differ; hydration errorsUse Zustand's persist middleware with SSR-safe initial state

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Render count audit: React Profiler per widget; no widget re-renders > 3 times per user action
  • Selector test: Unit test each Zustand selector with shallow equality check
  • State ownership diagram: Documented in wiki; reviewed during architecture meetings
  • Zustand DevTools: State changes visible in Redux DevTools (via devtools middleware)
  • Cross-widget test: Playwright test: change filter → verify only relevant widgets update
